chunk_summary: Guidance on feeding safety and texture modification for children with neurodisability or oral-motor dysfunction, emphasizing safe swallowing, energy-dense diets, and multidisciplinary care in cases of aspiration risk or growth failure.
learning_objectives: Identify signs of feeding and swallowing difficulties in children with neurodisability or oral-motor dysfunction.
Understand safe feeding practices, including texture modification and supportive positioning.
Recognize when to escalate care to specialists or multidisciplinary teams for feeding and nutrition management.
Learn strategies to optimize energy density in safe food textures for children with feeding challenges.
key_facts: Feeding difficulties may present as prolonged meals (>30 minutes), coughing, choking, or recurrent chest infections.
Safe feeding practices include upright posture, head midline, and slow pacing with pauses.
Thicker textures and smaller, frequent feeds reduce aspiration risk and feeding fatigue.
Nutrition rehabilitation can include calorie-dense foods like thick khichdi with ghee or nut pastes, considering age and allergy risks.
Link to RBSK and DEIC services in India for multidisciplinary assessment and follow-up.
tags_mesh: Feeding and Eating Disorders of Childhood
reference_quote: Suspect feeding or swallowing difficulty when meals routinely exceed 30 minutes, the child coughs or chokes with liquids, has a 'wet' voice after feeds, drools excessively, has recurrent chest infections, or has poor head and trunk control. (Source: NICE NG62)
document_key_topics: Energy-dense diets for children with feeding difficulties
